perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” shall mean all information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information shall also include all information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information in the public domain at the time of the disclosure, or subsequently comes within the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” may include information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information may also include information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information in the public domain at the time of the disclosure, or subsequently comes within the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing “shall mean” to “may include” makes the definition of 'Confidential Information' non-exclusive. This creates a direct conflict with Section 3, which mandates the return of 'all Confidential Information'. If the Receiving Party can argue that certain information doesn't fall under the 'may include' definition, they might refuse to return it, leading to disputes over compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION All information provided by the Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party. When the Receiving Party has finished reviewing the information provided by the Disclosing Party and has made a decision as to whet her or not to work with the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all information to the Disclosing Party without retaining any copies.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall mean" to "may include" in the definition of 'Confidential Information' creates a non-exclusive definition. This directly contradicts Section 3, which requires the return of "all Confidential Information". The Receiving Party could argue that certain information doesn't fall under the 'may include' definition and refuse to return it. This creates a clear conflict and makes enforcement uncertain, fitting the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees to return the majority of the Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all Confidential Information' to 'the majority of the Confidential Information,' there's now ambiguity about what constitutes 'the majority.' This contradicts the overall intent of the NDA, especially in Section 2(h), which requires the Receiving Party to 'Utilize the best efforts possible to protect and safeguard the Confidential Information from loss, theft, destruction, or the like.' It's unclear how one can protect information they are not required to return.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION The Receiving Party agrees to: (a) Receive and maintain the Confidential Information in confidence; (b) Examine the Confidential Information at its own expense; (c) Not reproduce the Confidential Information or any part thereof without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; (d) Not, directly or indirectly , make known, divulge, publish or communicate the Confidential Information to any person, firm or corporation without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; (e) Limit the internal dissemination of the Confidential Information and the internal disclosure of the Confid ential Information received from the Disclosing Party to those officers and employees, if any, of the Receiving Party who have a need to know and an obligation to protect it; (f) Not use or utilize the Confidential Information without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 3 (g) Not use the Confidential Information or any part thereof as a basis for the design or creation of any method, system, apparatus or device similar to any method, system, apparatus or device embodied in the Confidential Information unless expressly authorized in writing by Disclosing Party; a n d (h) Utilize the best efforts possible to protect and safeguard the Confidential Information from loss, theft, destruction, or the like.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'all Confidential Information' to 'the majority of the Confidential Information' introduces ambiguity, which directly contradicts Section 2(h)'s requirement to 'Utilize the best efforts possible to protect and safeguard the Confidential Information from loss, theft, destruction, or the like.' If the Receiving Party is not required to return all confidential information, it becomes unclear how they can fulfill their obligation to protect the information they are not returning, creating a direct conflict and inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: 4. NON-ASSIGNABLE This agreement shall be non-assignable by the Receiving Party unless prior written consent of the Disclosing Party is receiv ed. If this Agreement is assigned or otherwise transferred, it shall be binding on all successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. NON-ASSIGNABLE This agreement shall be assignable by the Receiving Party with prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. If this Agreement is assigned or otherwise transferred, it shall not be binding on all successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the meaning of 'non-assignable' creates a contradiction. Initially, Section 4 prevents assignment without consent. Now, it permits assignment with consent, but then contradicts itself stating if the Agreement is assigned it shall not be binding. This creates an issue with Section 7 which states 'This agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective heirs, personal representatives, successors and assigns'. Now it is unclear whether it is binding or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. BINDING NATURE OF AGREEMENT This agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective heirs, personal representatives, successors and assigns.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited assignment without consent, aligning with Section 7, which mandates the agreement's binding nature on successors and assigns. The changed text permits assignment with consent, but then states if the Agreement is assigned it shall not be binding. This directly contradicts Section 7, which states 'This agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective heirs, personal representatives, successors and assigns'. Therefore, there's a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek, but not obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing from 'may seek and obtain' to 'may seek, but not obtain' is contradiction. This renders Section 2 (USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION) useless, especially in sections where the Receiving Party agrees to safeguard the information and prevent its disclosure. It's illogical to prevent injunctive relief against disclosure when the core purpose is confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION The Receiving Party agrees to: (a) Receive and maintain the Confidential Information in confidence; (b) Examine the Confidential Information at its own expense; (c) Not reproduce the Confidential Information or any part thereof without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; (d) Not, directly or indirectly , make known, divulge, publish or communicate the Confidential Information to any person, firm or corporation without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; (e) Limit the internal dissemination of the Confidential Information and the internal disclosure of the Confid ential Information received from the Disclosing Party to those officers and employees, if any, of the Receiving Party who have a need to know and an obligation to protect it; (f) Not use or utilize the Confidential Information without the express written consent of Disclosing Party; Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 3 (g) Not use the Confidential Information or any part thereof as a basis for the design or creation of any method, system, apparatus or device similar to any method, system, apparatus or device embodied in the Confidential Information unless expressly authorized in writing by Disclosing Party; a n d (h) Utilize the best efforts possible to protect and safeguard the Confidential Information from loss, theft, destruction, or the like.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text from 'may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief' to 'may seek, but not obtain temporary injunctive relief'. This creates a direct conflict with Section 2, which requires the Receiving Party to 'safeguard the information and prevent its disclosure'. If a party cannot obtain injunctive relief against improper disclosure, it undermines the purpose of protecting the Confidential Information as outlined in Section 2. The change introduces a strong contradiction because it renders the confidentiality obligations in Section 2 unenforceable through injunctive relief, which is a standard legal remedy for breaches of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement sets forth all of the co venants, promises, agreements, conditions and understandings between the parties an d there are no covenants, promises, agreements or conditions, either oral or written, between them other than herein set forth.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement sets forth some of the co venants, promises, agreements, conditions and understandings between the parties an d there are covenants, promises, agreements or conditions, either oral or written, between them other than herein set forth.
perturbation.explanation: Changing “all of the” to “some of the”, creates a contradiction, making it unclear which agreements are binding and which are not. In the rest of the agreement there are conditions and understandings, so this amendment to Section 9 makes those conditions that are elsewhere in the document, less relevant or important. It would be contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This agreement sets forth all of the co venants, promises, agreements, conditions and understandings between the parties an d there are no covenants, promises, agreements or conditions, either oral or written, between them other than herein set forth. No subsequent alteration, amendment, change or addition to this Agreement shall be binding upon either party unless reduced in writing and signed by them.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the agreement contains *all* covenants, promises, agreements, conditions, and understandings. The changed text modifies this to state that the agreement contains *some* of these items. The contradicted text also asserts that the agreement sets forth *all* covenants, etc., and that no subsequent alteration is binding unless in writing. By changing 'all' to 'some' in the changed text, it directly contradicts both the original intent and the contradicted section, creating ambiguity about which terms are binding and which are not. This creates a clear and direct conflict.
